Is AI Getting Quietly Dumber? AIStupidLevel: A 24-Hour Watchdog for LLM Degradation

You've probably hit this before — yesterday the AI felt sharp, fixed your bug without you even asking, and threw in a few extra cleanups along the way. Then today, same kind of problem, and suddenly it refuses to touch anything you didn't explicitly point at, starts going in circles, or just answers something unrelated. And you start wondering "wait, is AI getting quietly dumber?"

I think a lot of people have had this feeling, but it's hard to actually pin down. Is the provider messing with the model? Is today's problem just genuinely harder? Or is it all in your head? Without a number to point at, it just stays an urban legend.
So this post is about a site called
— the name pretty much spells out what it does. It runs round-the-clock tests against the major AI models and turns "is AI getting dumber" into an actual curve you can look at.
That's enough of a hook to write about it.
In short, AIStupidLevel is a third-party benchmark platform that
continuously monitors whether AI models are regressing

. Think of it like a system health check, except it's checking the "health" of AI models — flagging which ones have quietly started slipping. It runs 24/7 against 21 production AI models from 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM) and turns each model's current performance into a score on a dashboard.

Some people might wonder if this is run by some AI monitoring company. It's not — it's an open source project. So it leans toward the "third-party watchdog" role, which I think matters here. You don't want the people grading the models to also be selling them.
OK, with the basics out of the way, let's keep going.
Hold on — why would an AI "get dumber"?
I think a lot of people would push back here:
Doesn't the model get trained once and then frozen?
A few things are going on:
If you've worked with the API, you've probably seen version strings like
— that trailing date hints at a specific build. But when you pick a model inside Codex or similar tools, you don't see that level of detail. Some models don't even have versioned IDs, just a generic
. So you can't actually tell which build you're hitting, and a silent swap is really hard to notice.
To handle global peak traffic and save compute, providers sometimes compress the model (quantize it).
Once usage crosses a threshold, providers may cap how much compute each user can pull, which makes outputs feel worse.

When a new model is about to launch, providers sometimes shift compute away from the older model onto the newer one, and the older one starts feeling sluggish. From my own time hitting the API, I run into this a lot — the same prompt suddenly degrades to "kind of dumb" overnight, then a few days later the provider ships a stronger version, and shortly after, the old version goes back to normal.
I covered quantization more thoroughly in my earlier post
Want to Run an LLM Yourself? Understanding Model Parameters and Quantization So You Stop Picking the Wrong Model
— short version, it's a compression technique that saves resources but loses some detail.

An AI model isn't actually a frozen thing. Providers can silently update it, quantize it, throttle it, or migrate compute away from it — and any of those will degrade what you experience. The hard part is, you usually can't tell. That's why "is the AI getting quietly dumber" is a real question, not paranoia.
Lots of words above, but I haven't gotten to how AIStupidLevel actually tests. The core idea is simple:
It maintains a fixed set of tasks, throws them at each model on a schedule, and logs the scores.
Because model outputs are stochastic, it runs each task N times and takes the median, plus a 95% confidence interval.
Sounds simple, right? The devil is in the details.
Same question N times, because AI is non-deterministic

Right — and the test design leans into that. If you only ran each task once, the confidence in the score would be really low. So each task gets run at least N times (and N is 5 in AIStupidLevel's case), and the score is the median across runs. That way you're tracking the model's "typical performance" instead of getting fooled by a single lucky or unlucky roll.
AIStupidLevel doesn't just run one kind of test — it has several different suites taking turns, each watching for different things. A table is probably clearest:
147 coding problems, gauging overall coding ability
5–7 turn multi-turn dialogues, checking if long-conversation logic falls apart
Spins up a real Docker sandbox so the AI can actually run multi-step flows like execute-command, read-file, write-file
12 lightweight quick checks, first line of defense against anomalies

You can see the design: high-frequency quick scans plus low-frequency deep tests. The hourly canary plays sentinel — if something starts looking off, it sounds the alarm. The daily deep reasoning and tool-calling runs are the heavier full-body checkup.

The tool-calling suite is the one I find especially interesting. It actually spins up a Docker sandbox (think of it as an isolated mini-computer) and has the AI run real commands inside it, rather than just "verbally" claiming it can use tools. The results are way closer to what you actually feel when using AI to write code.
Scoring isn't just right vs wrong
So how does a single task get scored? This is another place where it gets pretty detailed. Instead of just checking correctness, it scores across multiple dimensions.

For coding problems, AIStupidLevel currently weights across 9 dimensions: correctness (40%), complexity (20%), code quality (15%), stability (10%), efficiency (5%), edge case handling (3%), debugging (3%), output format (2%), and safety (2%). Correctness clearly dominates, but even if the answer runs, you'll still lose points if it's a mess, misses edge cases, or spews garbage formatting.
How does it catch the moment a model starts getting dumber?
Just having a score isn't enough — the score naturally bounces around (because, as covered above, AI is non-deterministic). The real question is: is this drop a real regression, or just normal noise?
This is the technical core of the project. It uses an algorithm called

for this. CUSUM didn't come from AI research — it's an old quality-control method from manufacturing. The idea is to keep accumulating the gap between observed performance and the baseline. Once the accumulated gap crosses a threshold, you call it: this isn't noise, something actually changed.
On top of CUSUM, it also runs statistical significance testing (checking whether the difference is statistically meaningful, p-value below 0.05) as a second pass to keep false alarms down.
The real win here: with this statistical machinery, it can detect a degradation
of it starting, rather than waiting until people are venting on social media that "AI got dumber." The system has 29 different warning categories built in, covering all kinds of anomaly patterns.
So what does the score look like? (Stupid Meter)
You can see the dashboard the moment you open the site.

Each model has a live 0–100 score next to it — the higher, the better it's performing right now, basically a "smartness gauge" for AI models. Next to the score is a status tag telling you which of four states the model is in: STABLE, VOLATILE (jittering), DEGRADED (already worse), or RECOVERING (climbing back up).

Beyond the current score, it also lays out each model's historical curve, so you can compare scenarios like "this model was rock-solid last week, why is it jumping around this week." Combined with the 29 warning categories I mentioned, it's pretty quick to tell which models are stable right now and which are acting weird.

So in practice it feels more like a stock-trading dashboard — you're not looking at a static ranking, you're checking "right at this moment, which model is worth using." If you're still wrestling with which AI tool to pick, my earlier post
Which AI Coding Tool Should You Pick in 2026?
A quick word on Smart Router
Beyond the monitoring side, AIStupidLevel also ships a feature called
, which I think is a pretty interesting extension of the project.

In short, it's an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint (meaning code you wrote against OpenAI barely needs to change). You drop each provider's API key into it (stored with AES-256 encryption), point your base URL at it, and it routes each request to whichever model is currently in the best shape based on its live monitoring.
It currently offers six routing strategies — you just set the
field in your API call to one of these and it picks dynamically:
: overall pick — whichever model has the best combined score right now
: picks whichever model is best at coding right now
: picks the cheapest model above the quality bar
: picks the fastest model above the quality bar
, it uses whichever strategy you've set as your default. And if you pass a specific model name (like

), it pins that model directly and skips the router. So when a model quietly starts slipping and Smart Router catches it, it auto-routes traffic to a sibling model that's still healthy. Pretty useful if you actually want to wire AI into a product.
OK, lots of words — let's wrap.
(MIT License) third-party benchmark site that monitors 21 production AI models across 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM) 24 hours a day
— each task runs 5 times, score is the median plus a 95% confidence interval, across four test suites (high-frequency canary + low-frequency deep tests) on a rotation
plus statistical testing to catch a model quietly degrading within hours
, which auto-routes API traffic to whichever model is in the best shape based on the live monitoring
Next time you feel like the AI suddenly got dumber, don't jump straight to blaming yourself — pop this site open and you might actually find the evidence.

This post walks through AIStupidLevel — a third-party benchmark site that keeps a 24-hour eye on 21 production AI models across 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM). It throws coding, reasoning, and tool-calling tasks at them every few hours, runs each task 5 times to take the median with a 95% confidence interval, and uses the CUSUM change-point detection algorithm to catch when a model is quietly getting dumber. We'll cover why models degrade (silent updates, quantization), how the four test suites work, how to read the Stupid Meter, and the bundled Smart Router that auto-picks the best model for you.
